However, it seems like one of the major issues is that it struggles with word overlap and ambiguity, especially when dealing with words that are really similar or have double letters. But overall, people seem to be happy with the update and feel that it's getting close to being as good as some other popular keyboards like Gboard. Interestingly, one user points out that the original iPhone keyboard layout was actually designed to account for finger movement and overlap, kind of like FUTO Swipe. And funnily enough, another user mentions that they used this same swipe technique on iOS with Grammarly's keyboard, which is pretty cool. groq_explain:
So it looks like there's a new keyboard layout out called FUTO Swipe. The idea behind it is to make keyboard typing faster by swiping from one letter to the next, which is supposed to be easier and faster than tapping on individual keys. The problem with existing swiping keyboards is that they have trouble with words that sound similar, like when you're trying to type a word with double letters and it gets confused.

The FUTO Swipe keyboard seems to be designed to address this issue by optimizing the keyboard layout to minimize words that look the same when you're swiping. From what people are saying, it seems to be working pretty well, with one user saying it's even better than Gboard, Google's keyboard, which they were previously using.

author: turtleyacht
title: Jerry's Map
groq_label: Jerry's Map uses over 100 custom deck cards to systematize the creation of a 2D virtual world art project.
groq_discussion: So the commenters are all super excited about Jerry's Map, which is this amazing project where some guy creates these sprawling, intricate maps using a deck of instructions. People are comparing it to outsider art, like Henry Darger's creations, and saying it's some of the most Borgesian thing they've seen. Some people are sharing their own experiences making imaginary maps and worlds when they were younger, like this one guy who made maps of his imaginary world on posterboard and even programmed a printer to print them out. Others are talking about how the system used to create the map is pretty fascinating, and how it gives the map a sense of being a system that Jerry is observing and evolving over time. There's also some nostalgia for the days of finding smaller documentaries on Vimeo, and a mention of using AI to post-process the maps to make them even better. Some people are drawing parallels to other projects, like the scale model of NYC being built by a truck driver, and even referencing some obscure books and music. Overall, it's a pretty lively discussion with a lot of people sharing their own stories and connections to the project.
groq_explain:
You know how sometimes you get a hobby that just takes on a life of its own? That's what happened with this guy, Jerry. Back in 1963, he started doodling a map of an imaginary city in his spare time. It was just a fun thing to do while he was working a boring job. But over the years, he kept adding to it and eventually it became this huge project.

It's called "Jerry's Map" and it's basically a two-dimensional world that's made up of over 4,000 little panels. Each panel is an 8x10 inch square of paper and they're all connected together in a big circle. But here's the cool thing about it - Jerry doesn't just draw on the panels once and leave it at that. He has these special cards that he draws from to guide him as to where to draw next.

It's kind of like a game, I guess. He'll draw a card and it'll tell him where to draw on the map next, and how to draw it. Sometimes the cards will give him instructions to change up what he's already drawn, so the map is always evolving. It's been going on for decades, and Jerry has a whole blog about the process that you can still read online.

It's pretty amazing to see how much work goes into this thing. And there's even a subreddit dedicated to people who are inspired by Jerry's Map and are creating their own similar projects.

groq_explain:

author: goranmoomin
title: Vulnerability reports are not special anymore
groq_label: Vulnerability reports are becoming overwhelming due to a surge in reports from language models and spammers.
groq_discussion: So I've been catching up on this article and the comments about it. A lot of people are saying that with all the tools and language models available now, vulnerability reports have lost their special status. They're seeing a lot of spam reports, and even the real security researchers are getting tired of them being dismissed. Some comments are saying that this is a temporary thing, though - as language models get better at reporting and fixing bugs, they'll eventually be good at finding bugs before they're even shipped, and the flood of bug reports will die down. Others are saying that this is just an engineering problem and that we need to overhaul our software practices to minimize bugs in the first place, not just rely on models to find them. A few people are pointing out that the cost of human time vs. language model time is a big reason why LLMs are finding more vulnerabilities, but that it's not necessarily a bad thing. Some people are also questioning whether vulnerability reports were ever really special in the first place, and wondering if we're just getting a dose of our own medicine with all the spam reports. There are a few clever insights, too - like one person suggesting that AI's "jagged intelligence" might actually be a weakness in certain scenarios, and that skilled humans can still find issues that LLMs struggle with. Oh, and there's a discussion about incentives and the fact that people are expecting the same treatment for reporting trivial vulns as they do for critical ones.
groq_explain:
So, people are saying that vulnerability reports aren't as exciting or special as they used to be. They're kind of getting overrun by spam and automated reports from language models (LLMs). These models can find bugs pretty easily, and some people are even getting reports from LLMs that are just finding minor issues like bad CSS on their website.

A lot of actual security researchers are complaining that their reports are being taken less seriously because they're being flooded with fake or automated reports. Some people are even seeing legitimate researchers get dismissed with a cease and desist.

One guy thinks this is all temporary, though. He thinks as soon as most of the bugs are fixed, the LLMs will be good at finding bugs before they even happen, and the stream of reports will die down.

Others are taking it as an opportunity to overhaul software development practices to make it harder to create bugs in the first place. Some are talking about new languages or better ways to check for security issues like authentication problems.

And then there's the basic fact that LLMs are finding way more bugs than humans because they're cheaper and more efficient. One guy is saying that we just need to give people the time and resources to actually find and fix these problems.

author: bewal416
title: Stealing Is a Skill
groq_label: The idea that copying can be a creative stepping stone to originality is being used to justify copyright infringement, but it's actually a nuanced debate that depends on intent, context, and the line between artistic tradition and commercial exploitation.
groq_discussion: You're going to love this, I just caught up on the comments on that article about stealing being a skill. It seems like people are having a real discussion about the line between paying homage and blatant copying. Some commenters feel like there's a difference between being inspired by someone else's work and just outright copying, like in the case of the Air Force 1s redesign. Others are talking about how copying can actually be a learning experience, especially when it comes to web design, where you can see how the original was built and try to recreate it. There are some disagreements about whether this is just plain copying or if it's a form of tribute and building on what's come before. Some people even brought up art and community traditions, where copying is seen as a way of paying respects rather than trying to one-up someone else. It's a really nuanced topic, and it seems like everyone has their own opinions on it. One commentator even shared a quote from Mikhail Kalashnikov, who said that understanding what already exists is key to creating something new. It's definitely an interesting conversation.
groq_explain:
So there's this article about "Stealing Is a Skill" and it's causing quite a stir. Apparently, the author is suggesting that copying or recreating someone else's work, especially in design and web development, can actually be a valuable learning experience and a way to develop your own skills.

Some people are defending the idea, saying that it's not just about copying, but about learning from the original work and using it as a foundation to create something new. They're pointing out that, in copywriting, copying another writer's work is actually a useful exercise for improving your own writing skills.

Others, however, are strongly against this idea, saying that it's basically just copying someone else's work without permission, which is essentially theft. They're drawing a line between borrowing inspiration and copying someone else's work verbatim, often for commercial gain. One person even referenced an article by Dan Mall that explores this idea in a more inspirational way.

People are also linking to other projects like Shrubbery, which adds Rhombus syntax to Racket. The language is designed to be both approachable for everyday use, with a readable indentation syntax, and uniquely customisable with an open API.

There's been some discussion about the language's performance, with one commenter mentioning that it may not be the fastest around. And there's a bit of debate going on about formal language theory, with one person wondering why designers wouldn't adopt a simpler syntax like Rhombus's shrubbery notation instead of using complex notions like LR(k) grammars. Some people are even asking about the importance of yet another programming language, but it seems like there's a lot of enthusiasm around Rhombus. Oh, and someone also pointed out a grammatical error in some text about modern programming languages, but that's about it in terms of criticism. Overall, it's looking like Rhombus is getting a great response from the community.
groq_explain:
So, there's this new programming language called Rhombus Language 1.0. It's built on top of Racket, which is another programming language. Apparently, Rhombus is designed to be pretty approachable and easy to use for everyday tasks, with a syntax that's pretty readable. One of the cool things about it is that it uses something called an "open-compiler API" which allows users to customize the language and make it work the way they need it to.

The `…` operator they have in Rhombus looks like it's pretty handy, it can work with nested data structures and can kind of do some of the same things as a `map` operation. It's actually defined as a macro, not a built-in thing, so that's pretty cool.

I've also seen that this guy Matthew Flatt did a video on Rhombus and showed how you can use it to define custom macros without needing things like s-expressions. There's also been some discussion about something called Shrubbery, which is just adding the Rhombus syntax to the Racket language.

author: givinguflac
title: Usbliter8: an A12/A13 SecureROM Exploit
groq_label: A vulnerability dubbed Usbliter8 has been found in Apple's A12 and A13 chip SecureROM, potentially allowing exploitation of certain iPhone models' USB DMA functionality.
groq_discussion: So, I've been catching up on the comments about this Usbliter8 exploit. It's really interesting - apparently, it's not going to allow an attacker to gain full control of an iOS device unless there's another exploit like BPR, which was added in iOS 14 for extra security. This is considered a big deal because it's an A12/A13 SecureROM exploit and is considered a low-level hardware/firmware hole that can't be patched. A lot of people are thinking about what this means for the future of jailbreaking, particularly for older iPhone models like the XR, XS, 11, and SE 2nd gen, and a bunch of iPads, which are still running iOS 27 beta. Some people are nostalgic for the old days of jailbreaking, when it allowed you to unlock all sorts of handy features. Others are thinking critically about the vulnerability, trying to figure out if it could be used to create patches or if it's entirely unfixable. One person made a really clever analogy about packets being passed around like balls on a cliff, which I thought was pretty cool.
groq_explain:
It looks like some hackers have found a vulnerability in the Apple A12 and A13 chip, called SecureROM. They're saying it's a low-level security issue that can't be patched, and it affects several iPhone models. What's cool is that it's not a full-on jailbreak, but more like a starting point that could lead to further exploration.

Apparently, the exploit is connected to how the USB controller in these chips works, and it allows someone to access secure storage on the device. But the good news is that it's not a vulnerability that an attacker can use to gain full access to the device without another exploit, so it's not a complete backdoor.

Now, I know what you're thinking - this sounds like the old days of jailbreaking, when you could do all sorts of cool stuff on your iPhone. Unfortunately, it seems like a lot of app developers are dropping support for older iOS versions, so devices that are affected by this exploit might not be able to run the latest software.

It's hard to say what this will lead to, but it's definitely an interesting development. The people who discovered the exploit are saying that it's an unfixable vulnerability, so it's not like Apple can just push out a fix to patch it. That's got a lot of people talking, and I'm curious to see what happens next.

author: dakshgupta
title: PR spam today looks like email spam in the early 2000s

groq_discussion: Commenters are weighing in on the issue of PR spam, with one person noting a difference between email spam and PR spam, saying that email spam was more focused on sender reputation and domains, not individual users. Another commenter points out that GitHub has recently added configurable PR limits for maintainers to help address the problem. Some have also shared personal experiences, with one person sharing a Twitter thread about their work fighting spam in the early 2000s, while another has a clever suggestion to let maintainers decide how to use donated token credits. There's a bit of disagreement on how to approach the issue, with some saying that contributors are mostly just enthusiastic newcomers who can be taught to use coding agents, while others see it as a serious problem that needs to be addressed. groq_explain:
So apparently, people are comparing the current state of PR (pull request) spam to the good old days of email spam back in the 2000s. It's a bit like that, but with some differences. Mainly, with email spam, it was about the sender's reputation and the organizations running the email servers that had to deal with it, not individual users. Here, PR spam is a different beast because it's more about individual users and maintainers of open-source projects who are receiving all these requests.

Some people are suggesting ways to address this issue, like setting limits on PRs or having contributors meet with maintainers before their first PR is merged. Others think that it's not as bad as it seems and that many contributors genuinely want to share their work and learn from others.

author: jxmorris12
title: Why eval startups fail (2025)

One guy built his own eval tool and noted that most eval startups are actually observability platforms for LLM inference, which sounds like they're trying to sell a service that's way too broad. Another commenter compared evals to glorified integration tests and expressed that they're basically not worth investing in.

There's also some skepticism about the demand for evals, with one guy saying that most companies didn't pay for eval work even back in the 2010s. But another commentator thinks that there will be a huge demand for evaling individual systems as governments start to set criteria for LLM usage, and that's where the value will be.

One clever insight came from a commenter who pointed out that an eval is basically a function that inverts the cost of AI and uses it to train better models. He said that instead of spending money on eval startups, users should focus on fine-tuning their own models.

Some people pointed out that evals struggle to keep up with the latest model performance benchmarks, which is a huge problem for companies that rely on the latest and greatest. Another person thought that eval startups fail because they're trying to sell a service rather than a toolchain for verification, which is a really interesting point.

Overall, it seems like eval startups are facing a lot of challenges, from changing customer demands to difficulties keeping up with the fast-paced AI landscape.
groq_explain:
So, it seems like there's a lot going on in the evaluation tool space for Large Language Models (LLMs). Some companies are trying to create these all-encompassing platforms for observing and evaluating how well an LLM is performing, but it seems like most people think that's not a good use of resources.

A lot of people are saying that evaluation tools are just glorified integration tests, and that they're not a particularly exciting or profitable space to be in. In fact, someone points out that they used to work on something like this in the 2010s, but it didn't seem worth investing in.

However, there is a growing demand for evaluation tools that can be used to assess the quality of individual systems, rather than just observing how they're performing in real-time. This is partly because governments are starting to set criteria for how LLMs can be used in public settings, and they're going to need independent evaluators to make sure everything is up to snuff.

One person makes the point that evaluation tools are really just about figuring out how to make better models, and that's a more productive use of resources than just trying to make users happy with slightly better performance. They also mention that a lot of people working on eval startups end up going to work for companies like Frontier Labs or fine-tuning startups.

Lastly, some people are pointing out that evaluation tools need to be constantly updated to reflect the latest model changes, and that's a challenge in itself.

author: caerbannogwhite
title: Statistics that live in your SQL
groq_label: lm() gives you the coefficient table in a SQL query, complete-case filtered, and cross-verified against R on cars and mtcars to four decimals.
groq_discussion: No comments.
groq_explain:
So I've been catching up on this cool new extension for this open-source database called DuckDB. It's called the-stats-duck and it does all sorts of statistical analysis within SQL, which is really convenient. 

Normally, when you're working with data, the first thing you'd want to do is take a look at it. The-stats-duck has a function called meta() that does that for you. It takes a dataset and gives you a bunch of information about each column, like what type of data it is and how many missing values there are. Plus, you can use it in SQL queries, so you can join it with other data, filter it, or even use it in complex queries.

One of the most impressive things about the-stats-duck is that it can do linear regression within SQL. So you can write a SQL query that does a linear regression and gets all the results, like the coefficients and the model-level line, without having to leave the database. 

Another really useful function is bootstrap(). It allows you to calculate confidence intervals by brute force, which is handy if you're not assuming normality. And with its power, you can do things like calculating mean and median intervals.

The-stats-duck also has a simple drawing function that allows you to create visualizations within SQL. It's like a mini version of ggplot, and it compiles to Vega-Lite. In this latest release, they've added new functionality, like different types of plots, smooth lines, and per-layer statistics.

It's really exciting because it means that you can do all sorts of complex statistical analysis within SQL, without having to leave the database, and even get visualizations out of it. It's super useful and it runs anywhere DuckDB runs, including in the browser.
